schools.summary: Gill: Sheba's chance presence was ordered by providence to fulfil the threat that the sword should not depart from David's house.
Henry roots the revolt in the Israel-Judah quarrel and in the crowd's fickleness, 'Today Hosanna, to-morrow Crucify.'
Ellicott reads Bichri as a patronymic (the Bichrite, of Becher's clan) and 'every man to his tents' as a formal signal of revolt.
Clarke and Wesley stress Sheba's Benjamite (Saulide) grudge and the contempt in calling David 'the son of Jesse.'

debates.question: Is 'Bichri' a personal name or a patronymic?

sides.position: a proper name, following the ancient versions
a patronymic, 'the Bichrite,' of the clan of Becher

themes: providence and the sword on David's house

schools.summary: Keil parses the 'pregnant' construction of dabaq (clave and followed); Ellicott charts the reversal by which Judah grows fiercely loyal and Israel rebellious.
Gill notes it was those who had met David on the road who now departed from him.
Wesley reads 'every man of Israel' as the generality of those present, not literally all.
Keil and Ellicott parse 'widowhood of life' and trace the tragedy to the system of polygamy.
Clarke lays out David's fourfold dilemma: he could neither divorce, punish, keep, nor remarry them, so he kept them in comfortable seclusion.
Henry: David had multiplied wives contrary to the law, and now must in duty, prudence, and justice put them away.
Gill locates David's palace in the fort of Zion, the city of David.

sides.position: widowhood of the living, i.e. while their husband still lived

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott judge the commissioning of Amasa a very imprudent act that gravely offended the veteran Joab.
Henry frames it as 'Amasa's fall just as he began to rise' and rebukes a loyalty that is cheap and easy.
Clarke reads the order as a formal investiture of Amasa with command.
Gill: by this order David declared Amasa general, as he had promised.
Keil handles the Chethib/Keri variant and the 'tear out our eye' figure; Ellicott infers Amasa exceeded his orders into Benjamin, casting doubt on his fitness.
Wesley: the people were war-weary, or the soldiers preferred Joab to their new general.

debates.question: Why did Amasa tarry beyond the three days?

sides.position: he exceeded his instructions, straying into Benjamin, showing unfitness (and, Joab hints, disloyalty)
the war-weary people or the Joab-loyal soldiers were slow to muster

themes: the eye as the precious thing

schools.summary: Gill: David, displeased with Joab over Absalom, deliberately speaks to Abishai, and corrects Josephus for saying otherwise.
Ellicott and Keil read David's determination to pass over Joab and weigh the disputed final clause about the 'eye.'
Wesley: David addressed Abishai lest Joab recover his place and Amasa be aggrieved and the royal promise questioned.

debates.question: How should the difficult closing clause be rendered?

sides.position: 'pluck/tear out our eye,' i.e. do us serious harm
'overshadow our eye,' i.e. cause anxiety or hide from us (LXX)

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies Joab's men, the Cherethites and Pelethites, and the 'mighty men' as David's old outlaw-band heroes.
Gill notes Joab, though removed as general, still commanded a regiment.
Wesley: Joab went out as a 'reformade,' watching an opportunity to do what he designed.

themes: the household troops loyal to Joab

schools.summary: Gill sets Gibeon five miles from Jerusalem as the appointed rendezvous where Amasa took command.
Keil untangles the circumstantial clauses and is struck that Joab appears as commander; Ellicott says the verse exists to show how Joab could stab unsuspected.
Clarke thinks Joab stumbled and the sword fell, then was picked up innocently; Wesley thinks the fall was deliberately contrived.

debates.question: Did the sword fall by accident or by design?

sides.position: accidentally, when Joab stumbled, then innocently picked up
by deliberate contrivance, arranged to fall and be picked up unsuspected

schools.summary: Gill: Joab's 'Art thou in health, my brother?' was spoken with every air of pleasantry and good humour.
Keil explains the beard-grasp as a customary friendly welcome and names Joab's jealousy as the sole motive.
Henry: Joab acted treacherously under pretence of friendship, calling Amasa 'brother' while aiming at his heart.
Gill: Amasa, unsuspecting, thought Joab was merely resheathing the fallen sword, and so was off his guard.
Wesley reads Amasa's fatal trust and explains why his men did not avenge him; Clarke shows 'fifth rib' means the abdomen, 'he ripped up his belly.'
Ellicott glosses 'fifth rib' as abdomen and links Abner's death; Keil notes the single unrepeated stroke and Joab's jealousy.
Gill: the herald was posted to reconcile the arriving people to the murder and keep them marching under Joab.
Clarke and Wesley unpack the slogan that yokes loyalty to Joab with loyalty to David against the 'perfidious rebel' Amasa.
Ellicott exposes Joab's cynical haste to make himself impregnable and names jealousy and ambition, as with Abner, as his motive.
Gill: still living, Amasa rolled in his own blood in the highway.
Keil and Ellicott explain the crowd's paralysis at the sight and the expedient removal and covering of the body; Clarke confirms men can live hours after such a wound.
Wesley: the cloth was to hide the crime, but covering blood cannot stop its cry to God for vengeance.
Henry: the concealed crime still cries in God's ear; the wicked feel safe only if it is hidden from the world.

themes: the crowd paralysed by the sight

schools.summary: Gill: once Amasa was removed into the field and hidden under a cloth, the halted people marched on.
Keil carries the narrative north, identifying Abel-Beth-maachah with the village of Abil-el-Kamh near Lake Huleh.

themes: the march resumes over a crime

schools.summary: Gill: the received sense makes Sheba, not Joab, the one who passed through the tribes and fled to Abel.
Wesley and Clarke locate Abel-Beth-maachah in the far north and identify the Berites with men of Beeroth of Benjamin.
Ellicott identifies Abel (Abel-maim) at the extreme north and admits the Berites are an otherwise unknown clan.
Henry: 'One bad man will find or make more'; the city that harbours a traitor is justly attacked, as is the heart that harbours rebellious lusts.

debates.question: Who 'went through all the tribes of Israel'?

sides.position: Sheba, fleeing and stirring sedition (the received view)

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott describe the rampart/mounds raised in the moat or trench between the walls; Ellicott corrects the name to Abel-Beth-maachah.
Wesley takes the 'bank' as a raised work for battering or shooting; Clarke argues the word means a battering engine or tower, not a bank.

debates.question: What is the 'bank' (solelah) raised against the city?

sides.position: an earthen bank or rampart raised in the trench
a battering engine or siege-tower, not a bank

themes: the city on the edge of storm

schools.summary: Gill likens her to the woman of Tekoah, supposes she governed the city, and praises her proven wisdom.
Keil infers from her words that Abel had long been celebrated for the wisdom of its people.
Wesley: none of the men would treat, but one wise woman saved the city; 'souls know no difference of sex.'
Henry marvels that a lone woman's wisdom saved the city while the stupefied elders did nothing.

themes: wisdom knows no difference of sex
the treasure in the weaker vessel

schools.summary: Gill: Joab drew near on his men's report that a woman on the wall wished to speak with him.
Keil explains the singular 'I' with a plural predicate: she speaks for the city, identifying herself with the citizens, as the figure 'mother' also shows.
Gill takes it as an ancient proverbial saying among the sages.
Clarke admits the proverb is obscure but supplies Abel's fame for arbitration and hears a rebuke to Joab; Wesley expands the city's renown for counsel.
Ellicott prefers 'Abel became proverbial for wisdom' but records the Targum's tie to the law of offering peace before war (Deut. 20:10).

debates.question: What does the proverb 'ask counsel at Abel' mean?

sides.position: simply that Abel was proverbially renowned for wise counsel and arbitration
a pointed reminder of the law to offer a city peace before besieging it (Deut. 20:10)

themes: the law of offering peace before war

schools.summary: Gill: she speaks for a city of peaceable, faithful men who never joined Absalom's rebellion.
Clarke and Wesley read 'mother in Israel' as a chief mother-city and give full weight to the plea against destroying 'the inheritance of the LORD.'
Henry admires her ingenious reasoning that destroying the town would weaken the nation God chose for his heritage.

themes: wasting God's cities as an affront to heaven

schools.summary: Gill: Joab's doubled 'far be it' shows how detestable to him was the thought of destroying the city.
Keil says her reproaches impressed Joab; Ellicott (with the Speaker's Commentary) draws the portrait of a politic, restrained, never needlessly cruel schemer.

themes: the danger of the reasonable man

schools.summary: Gill: Joab professes no ill will to the city, only to the one rebel it harbours.
Keil and Ellicott explain 'Mount Ephraim' as a range extending south into Benjamin, home region of the Benjamite Sheba.
Wesley thinks the woman a governess (like Deborah) who knew the citizens' temper; Clarke infers her great sway and that beheading was the penalty for a state rebel.
Henry: the single condition of peace is surrender of the traitor, as the sinner finds peace only by casting away the beloved lust.

themes: sin as the traitor to be surrendered
a woman in government like Deborah
Sheba the Benjamite of Mount Ephraim

schools.summary: Gill: she convened the chief inhabitants and by a wise, prudent speech won their unanimous consent.
Keil renders 'with her wisdom' the counsel Joab accepted; Ellicott notes the ending echoes the chapter's opening cry, the army dismissed to their homes.
Wesley: she set before them certain ruin if they refused and sure deliverance if they complied.
Henry: much mischief is prevented when parties understand one another; wisdom is confined to no rank or sex.

themes: peace by the surrender of one rebel

schools.summary: Gill weighs whether Joab was ever displaced; either way, after crushing the revolt he stands over all the host.
Clarke and Wesley: Joab murdered Amasa and seized command, and David dared not call him to account without risking civil war.
Keil explains the closing list mirrors 2 Sam. 8:16 but belongs to a later period, adding new offices.
Henry: Joab was too great to be displaced; let all serve public duties as faithful servants of the Son of David.

themes: faithful service to the Son of David

schools.summary: Gill: the office over the tribute was new, covering both home revenue and the tribute of conquered nations.
Clarke likens Adoram to a Chancellor of the Exchequer and Jehoshaphat to a registrar of public events.
Ellicott: 'tribute' is rather the forced-labour levy, absent from 2 Sam. 8:16, a mark of Oriental despotism introduced late and expanded under Solomon.

themes: the new office of the levy

schools.summary: Gill: Sheva was either the earlier scribe or his successor in a continuing office.
Clarke glosses the scribe simply as the king's secretary.
Ellicott identifies Sheva with the Seraiah of 2 Sam. 8:17 and notes nearly all these officers match the earlier list; Keil sees two lists of different periods.

themes: the altar at the centre of the realm

schools.summary: Gill canvasses the disputed identity of Ira, perhaps a Gileadite friend of David from his time in Gilead.
Clarke reads cohen ledavid as 'a priest to David,' a domestic chaplain, like the seers the kings kept.
Ellicott keeps 'cohen' and notes the office, once held by David's own sons, passed to a stranger after the disasters in his household.

debates.question: What was Ira's office as 'chief ruler' (cohen) about David?

sides.position: a priest or domestic chaplain to the king
a prince or chief civil counsellor (Chaldee 'rab')

themes: David's sons replaced by a stranger
the sword not departing from David's house
